fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a traffic throttling library built in Rust that devs can drop into any service to control how fast work gets processed. The basic idea is you configure an allowance — like '50 requests per second' or '2 per minute' — and then ask the library 'can this go through?' and it says yes or no, and if no it tells you exactly how long to wait before retrying. We also need it to handle bulkier workloads where you want to admit like 5 or 10 units at once, not just one at a time.

One big thing: it needs to be fully testable without depending on the system clock — remember how we solved that in the auth middleware last quarter? Same kind of pattern, the clock needs to be injectable so tests are deterministic.

Also need per-key isolation, so different tenants or endpoints each get their own independent budget under one shared config. And there should be a way to clean up stale keys periodically so memory doesn't grow forever.

Finally, a 'state reporting' mode where admitted requests also reveal how much budget is left — useful for dashboards or client hints.

All durations should be in nanoseconds internally. The adapter layer should read a single JSON command from stdin and write plain text lines to stdout. Exact output format details are in the test cases but basically think 'check allow', 'check deny wait_ns=...', that kind of thing.

trigger_keywords: quota from per_second per_minute per_hour
burst size default value when not overridden
what is the default burst when building a time-based quota
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For time-based quotas (per_second, per_minute, per_hour), the default burst_size equals the count supplied (e.g. per_second=50 gives burst_size=50). The replenish_interval_ns is the time base in nanoseconds divided by the count. burst_replenish_all_ns equals replenish_interval_ns multiplied by burst_size.

trigger_keywords: zero period rejection
invalid period error format
what happens when with_period_ms is 0
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A zero replenishment period is invalid. The system must output exactly 'error=invalid_period\n' (a single line) for quota_build when with_period_ms is 0 or when a new quota with zero total replenishment time is supplied. No quota properties are printed.

trigger_keywords: virtual clock starting value
initial clock time in simulate
what time does the clock start at in simulation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The virtual clock starts at exactly zero nanoseconds at the beginning of every simulate action. advance_ms ops accumulate additively and the output reports the absolute total in nanoseconds: 'advance now_ns=<total_ns>'.

trigger_keywords: batch larger than burst capacity
check_n impossible request error format
what is reported when n exceeds burst size
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When check_n or check_key_n is called with n greater than the quota's burst_size, the system outputs 'check_n n=<N> error=insufficient_capacity max=<burst_size>\n' (or the keyed prefix equivalent). This is independent of clock time — no amount of waiting makes it admissible.

trigger_keywords: state middleware remaining capacity output format
remaining budget after admission
how is leftover burst shown in state reporting mode
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When middleware=state is active, every allowed single-unit check outputs 'check allow remaining=<n>\n' where <n> is the number of additional units that could still be admitted immediately after this one, without any clock advancement. Denied checks still output 'check deny wait_ns=<n>\n' with no remaining field.

trigger_keywords: retain_recent eviction condition
when is a key considered stale and removed
what makes a key eligible for eviction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: retain_recent evicts a key when its virtual arrival time has fallen back to the state a brand-new key would have — i.e. when enough time has elapsed since the last admission that the key's budget is fully replenished. A key is retained as long as its state is 'fresher' than a never-seen key. Output is always 'retain_recent ok\n'.

trigger_keywords: shrink_to_fit purpose and output
compact storage after eviction
what does shrink_to_fit do
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: shrink_to_fit compacts the keyed limiter's internal storage (e.g. shrinks an underlying Vec or HashMap allocation) after stale keys have been removed by retain_recent. It always outputs 'shrink_to_fit ok\n'. It does not itself evict any keys — retain_recent must be called first.

trigger_keywords: keys output format sorted order
how are keys listed in keys op
what delimiter separates keys in output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The keys op outputs 'keys <comma-separated-sorted>
' where keys are sorted lexicographically and joined by commas with no spaces. Example: 'keys bar,baz,foo
'. If there are no keys the output is 'keys 
' (keys followed by a space and nothing, or just the empty list).

trigger_keywords: allow_burst override behavior
how does allow_burst change replenish_interval
does overriding burst change the per-unit interval
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The allow_burst override changes only burst_size and consequently burst_replenish_all_ns (= replenish_interval_ns * burst_size). The replenish_interval_ns is never affected by allow_burst — it depends solely on the base rate. Example: per_hour=2 allow_burst=90 gives replenish_interval_ns=1800000000000, burst_size=90, burst_replenish_all_ns=162000000000000.

trigger_keywords: new quota spec with burst and all_per_ms
custom quota from burst and total time
how to build quota using new constructor with burst
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'new' quota spec takes {"burst": B, "all_per_ms": T} where T is the total time in milliseconds to replenish B units. replenish_interval_ns = (T * 1_000_000) / B. If T is zero this is rejected the same way as with_period_ms=0. allow_burst may additionally override the burst on top of this.

trigger_keywords: check_key_n output prefix format
keyed batch admission output format
what does check_key_n output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
check_key_n outputs 'check_key_n key=<K> n=<N> allow
' or 'check_key_n key=<K> n=<N> deny wait_ns=<w>
' or 'check_key_n key=<K> n=<N> error=insufficient_capacity max=<m>
'. The key= and n= fields always appear before the result word.

trigger_keywords: len output when empty
empty flag value format in len
what does len print when no keys exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The len op always outputs 'len count=<n> empty=<bool>\n'. The bool is literally 'true' or 'false' (lowercase). When count=0 the empty flag is 'true'; when count>=1 it is 'false'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'quota_info', 'quota_build', 'simulate'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we solved that in the auth middleware last quarter — same kind of pattern
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Injectable clock abstraction (a Clock trait/interface) that allows substituting a deterministic VirtualClock for the real wall-clock in tests, as described in the PRD's 'time source MUST be an injectable abstraction' constraint and referenced throughout the simulate action in the test cases.

context_pointers.vague_reference: clean up stale keys periodically so memory doesn't grow forever
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The retain_recent op (evicts keys whose state has fully replenished back to initial) combined with shrink_to_fit op (compacts internal storage allocation), as shown in feature4_3_retain_recent.json and feature4_4_shrink_to_fit.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: state reporting mode — useful for dashboards or client hints
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The middleware=state flag on the simulate action, which augments admitted single-unit check responses with 'remaining=<n>' showing how many more units could be admitted immediately, as defined in feature5_1_remaining_capacity.json and feature5_2_replenishment.json.

context_pointers.vague_reference: per-key isolation so different tenants or endpoints each get their own independent budget
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The limiter=keyed flag on the simulate action, which activates a KeyedRateLimiter where each string key maintains its own independent token-bucket state under a shared Quota, accessed via check_key and check_key_n ops.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the exact expected output format, or refer to the simulate op vocabulary section of the PRD for the full list of supported operations and their output lines.

feature1_1_time_based_quota.json.description: Construct a rate-limiting quota from a human-friendly rate expressed against a fixed time base (a count of allowed units per second, per minute, or per hour). For each construction the system reports three derived properties: the time interval after which a single consumed unit is replenished, the maximum burst (the count of units that may be consumed back-to-back before any replenishment is required), and the total time required to replenish a fully-drained burst budget. Equivalent rates expressed against different time bases yield proportional replenishment intervals (an hourly base divides into the minutely base by sixty, which divides into the per-second base by sixty). Durations are reported in nanoseconds.

cases.expected_output:
replenish_interval_ns=60000000000
burst_size=1
burst_replenish_all_ns=60000000000

replenish_interval_ns=20000000
burst_size=50
burst_replenish_all_ns=1000000000

feature1_2_burst_capacity.json.description: Start from a rate expressed against a time base and then independently raise the maximum burst budget to a larger value, decoupling the long-run average rate from the amount that may be consumed in a single spike. The reported maximum burst reflects the overridden value, the per-unit replenishment interval is unchanged from the base rate, and the time to replenish a fully-drained burst grows in proportion to the larger burst. Durations are reported in nanoseconds.

cases.expected_output:
replenish_interval_ns=1800000000000
burst_size=90
burst_replenish_all_ns=162000000000000

feature1_3_custom_period.json.description: Construct a quota directly from a replenishment period (the time to restore one unit) instead of a named time base, optionally raising the maximum burst. A strictly positive period yields a valid quota whose reported properties follow the same rules as time-based quotas. A zero period cannot describe a meaningful rate and is rejected; the equivalent rejection occurs when supplying a burst together with a zero total replenishment time. A rejected construction reports a single neutral error category line rather than a quota.

cases.expected_output:
replenish_interval_ns=86400000000000
burst_size=10
burst_replenish_all_ns=864000000000000
error=invalid_period

feature2_1_single_cell.json.description: Drive a single global rate limiter against a virtual clock and submit single-unit admission requests interleaved with explicit clock advances. The limiter admits requests while burst budget remains, then rejects further requests until enough time passes to replenish budget. A fresh limiter admits the very first request. Once the burst budget is drained a request is rejected and reports the wait time (in nanoseconds) until the next admission is possible; after enough time elapses the burst is restored and requests are admitted again, then rejected once the restored budget is consumed. Each clock advance reports the new absolute virtual time in nanoseconds.

cases.expected_output: check allow

check allow
advance now_ns=1000000
check allow
advance now_ns=2000000
check deny wait_ns=498000000
advance now_ns=1002000000
check allow
advance now_ns=1003000000
check allow
advance now_ns=1004000000
check deny wait_ns=496000000

feature2_2_wait_time.json.description: Verify that the wait time reported on a rejected admission is exact. After draining the burst budget, a further request is rejected and reports the precise remaining wait time. Advancing the virtual clock by exactly that reported wait time makes the next request succeed. Advancing by strictly less than the reported wait time leaves the limiter still rejecting, and the new rejection reports the smaller remaining wait. Wait times and absolute clock readings are reported in nanoseconds.

cases.expected_output:
check allow
check allow
check allow
check allow
check allow
check deny wait_ns=200000000
advance now_ns=200000000
check allow

check allow
check allow
check allow
check allow
check allow
check deny wait_ns=200000000
advance now_ns=199000000
check deny wait_ns=1000000

feature3_1_batch_check.json.description: Submit batched admission requests that ask to admit a specific number of units atomically (all-or-nothing). A batch of one behaves identically to a single-unit request. A batch is admitted only if the whole batch fits within the currently available budget; otherwise the batch is rejected and reports the wait time (in nanoseconds) until the whole batch could be admitted. Over time the budget replenishes and previously rejected batches become admissible, then are rejected again once the restored budget is consumed. Each clock advance reports the new absolute virtual time in nanoseconds.

cases.expected_output:
check_n n=1 allow
advance now_ns=1000000
check_n n=1 allow
advance now_ns=2000000
check_n n=1 deny wait_ns=498000000
advance now_ns=1002000000
check_n n=1 allow
advance now_ns=1003000000
check_n n=1 allow
advance now_ns=1004000000
check_n n=1 deny wait_ns=496000000

check_n n=2 allow
check_n n=2 allow
advance now_ns=1000000
check_n n=2 deny wait_ns=499000000
advance now_ns=1001000000
check_n n=2 allow
advance now_ns=1002000000
check_n n=2 allow
advance now_ns=1003000000
check_n n=2 deny wait_ns=497000000

feature3_2_insufficient_capacity.json.description: Submit batched admission requests whose size exceeds the limiter's maximum burst capacity. Such a batch can never be admitted, regardless of how much time has elapsed, because it is larger than the most the limiter could ever hold at once. The limiter reports a distinct neutral error category indicating insufficient capacity, together with the maximum batch size that could ever conform. This outcome is independent of the virtual clock: advancing time does not make an over-capacity batch admissible.

cases.expected_output:
check_n n=15 error=insufficient_capacity max=5
check_n n=6 error=insufficient_capacity max=5
check_n n=7 error=insufficient_capacity max=5

feature4_1_per_key.json.description: Drive a keyed rate limiter against a virtual clock, where each distinct key carries its own independent budget under one shared quota. Admission decisions for one key never consume another key's budget. A fresh limiter admits the first request for every key. Draining one key's burst causes that key's subsequent requests to be rejected (with a reported wait time in nanoseconds) and later re-admitted as time replenishes its budget, while a different key, seen for the first time, still receives its full burst regardless of how much the clock has advanced. Each clock advance reports the new absolute virtual time in nanoseconds.

cases.expected_output:
check_key key=alpha allow
check_key key=beta allow

check_key key=alpha allow
advance now_ns=1000000
check_key key=alpha allow
advance now_ns=2000000
check_key key=alpha deny wait_ns=498000000
advance now_ns=1002000000
check_key key=alpha allow
advance now_ns=1003000000
check_key key=alpha allow
advance now_ns=1004000000
check_key key=alpha deny wait_ns=496000000
check_key key=beta allow
advance now_ns=1005000000
check_key key=beta allow
advance now_ns=1006000000
check_key key=beta deny wait_ns=498000000

feature4_2_key_count.json.description: Track how many distinct keys the keyed limiter is currently holding state for. A newly created limiter holds no keys and reports as empty. Each first-time admission request under a new key adds exactly one tracked key; repeated reporting shows the live key count growing by one per newly seen key and the empty flag turning false once at least one key is present.

cases.expected_output:
len count=0 empty=true
check_key key=foo allow
len count=1 empty=false
check_key key=bar allow
len count=2 empty=false
check_key key=baz allow
len count=3 empty=false

feature4_3_retain_recent.json.description: Reclaim memory in a keyed limiter by evicting keys whose state has become indistinguishable from a never-seen key. Three keys are admitted at staggered virtual times, then the clock is advanced by a varying amount before a reclamation pass is requested; a key is retained only while its state remains fresher than a brand-new key would be (i.e. its budget has not fully replenished back to the starting state). After reclamation, the surviving keys are reported in sorted order. With no extra advance all keys survive; as the clock advances further, keys drop out oldest-first until none remain.

cases.expected_output:
check_key key=foo allow
advance now_ns=200000000
check_key key=bar allow
advance now_ns=800000000
check_key key=baz allow
retain_recent ok
keys bar,baz,foo

check_key key=foo allow
advance now_ns=200000000
check_key key=bar allow
advance now_ns=800000000
check_key key=baz allow
advance now_ns=2000000000
retain_recent ok
keys bar,baz

feature4_4_shrink_to_fit.json.description: Compact the keyed limiter's internal storage after stale keys are evicted, keeping only the genuinely active keys. A long-lived key consumes a large batch (keeping its state fresh well into the future) while a short-lived key is admitted once. After the clock advances far enough that the short-lived key's state lapses, a reclamation pass followed by a storage-compaction request leaves only the long-lived key, and the reported live key count is one.

cases.expected_output:
check_key_n key=long-lived n=10 allow
check_key key=short-lived allow
advance now_ns=300000000
retain_recent ok
shrink_to_fit ok
len count=1 empty=false

feature5_1_remaining_capacity.json.description: Use a state-reporting mode in which every admitted request additionally reveals the remaining burst capacity (how many further units could still be admitted right now without replenishment). Starting from a full burst, each successive admission decreases the reported remaining capacity by one down to zero; the next request is rejected and reports the wait time (in nanoseconds) until capacity is available again.

cases.expected_output:
check allow remaining=3
check allow remaining=2
check allow remaining=1
check allow remaining=0
check deny wait_ns=250000000

feature5_2_replenishment.json.description: Use the state-reporting mode to observe that remaining burst capacity replenishes accurately over time for a quota built from a custom replenishment period and an explicit burst. Admissions decrease the reported remaining capacity to zero and then are rejected (reporting the wait time in nanoseconds). After advancing the virtual clock well beyond a full replenishment window, the reported remaining capacity is fully restored to the burst size and again decreases by one per admission down to zero before the next request is rejected.

cases.expected_output:
check allow remaining=1
check allow remaining=0
check deny wait_ns=90000000
advance now_ns=120000000000
check allow remaining=2
check allow remaining=1
check allow remaining=0
check deny wait_ns=90000000
